CHANGE RECORD 
 
When the quality of the products changes, the QuID is updated, and a row is added to this table.   
The third column specifies which sections or sub-sections have been updated.  The fourth column should 
mention the reason for updating the document . Column 5 should include the names of the author (s) 
doing the changes. Including the name in column 6 shows that the document has been validated by the 
TAC/MFC PQ leader or deputy (or TAC/MFC PQ leader or the deputy). This validation means th e 
document was checked for contents, formats and consistency with other documents.

KEY DEFINITIONS 
NWS shelf region 
(definition according to 
Wakelin et al. 2012) 
The region mask of Wakelin et al. (2012) is used to divide the model 
domain into 13 regions (see Figure 2).  
The NRT model domain includes the North West European shelf seas, as 
well as other regions such as part of the North East Atlantic Ocean, the 
Norwegian Trench, the Skagerrak and Kattegat. When we refer to the 
North West Shelf region, we only refer to a portion of the model 
domain. The North West Shelf region is defined by combining a number 
of regions from the Wakelin et al. (2012) region mask. These regions 
are: the Southern North Sea; Central North Sea; Northern North Sea; 
English Channel; Shetland Shelf; Irish Shelf; Irish Sea; and the Celtic Sea 
region.  
Climatology A long-term average, giving a representative value of a quantity. 
Light attenuation 
coefficient, Kd and Kd490 
The light attenuation coefficient describes how rapidly light is attenuated 
as it passes through water: a high attenuation coefficient means that light 
is absorbed or scattered quickly by particles or coloured material in the 
water and light cannot penetrate very far, while low attenuation means 
that the water is clear.  Attenuation varies with the wavel ength of the 
light: Kd490 refers specifically to attenuation of  light with wavelength 
490 nm, in the middle of the visible range, while Kd refers to average 
attenuation for all wavelengths. Kd i
